1.synthesis: John is given a measuring rod to measure the temple, altar, and worshippers — but not the outer court, 'for it is given over to the nations, and they will trample the holy city for forty-two months' (v.2). He is given two witnesses to prophesy for 1,260 days, clothed in sackcloth: 'These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth' (v.4). They can call down fire, shut the sky from rain, turn water to blood, and strike with plagues. When their testimony is complete, the beast from the abyss will kill them. Their bodies lie in the street of 'the great city that symbolically is called Sodom and Egypt, where their Lord was crucified' (v.8). After 3½ days, a breath of life enters them; they ascend to heaven in a cloud while their enemies watch. A great earthquake kills seven thousand; the survivors give glory to God. 'The second woe has passed' (v.14).
1.historical_context: The forty-two months / 1,260 days / 'time, times and half a time' (42 months = 1,260 days = 3½ years) appears five times in Revelation and twice in Daniel (Dan 7:25; 12:7). It derives from the period of Antiochus IV Epiphanes's desecration of the temple (167–164 BCE), which Daniel's 'time, times and half a time' encoded — three and a half years of tribulation, a broken seven, a limited period that falls short of completeness. The two witnesses' description as olive trees and lampstands draws from Zechariah 4:3–14, where Zerubbabel and Joshua (civil and priestly leadership) stand as olive trees beside the lampstand. The fire from their mouths recalls Elijah (who called fire from heaven, 1 Kings 18:38); shutting the sky recalls Elijah's drought (1 Kings 17:1); turning water to blood recalls Moses (Exod 7:17). The two witnesses embody the ministries of the prophets (Elijah) and the Torah (Moses) — the two figures who appeared with Christ at the transfiguration (Matt 17:3). The 'great city' of v.8 where the Lord was crucified is identified as simultaneously Sodom (wickedness), Egypt (oppression), and Jerusalem — a composite symbolic geography.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 11 closes the trumpet interlude and contains the seventh trumpet (v.15). The two witnesses occupy the majority of the chapter (vv.1–14), functioning as the definitive portrait of the church's prophetic witness in the era between Christ's resurrection and return. The sequence of the witnesses' story — prophesy, be killed, lie unburied, be resurrected, ascend — is a miniature passion-resurrection-ascension narrative, the church's story patterned on Christ's. The measuring of the temple (vv.1–2) and the protection of the inner court contrast with the outer court's trampling — a spatial symbol for the distinction between the sealed community (inwardly protected) and the world's dominion (externally unrestrained). The earthquake's aftermath (v.13: seven thousand killed, survivors give glory to God) is the first positive response to judgment in the book — a hint of repentance before the seventh trumpet's final announcement.
1.theological_application: Gill on the two witnesses: 'These two witnesses are designed to point at some particular persons, or set of persons, who should in those times publicly give witness to the truth of the Gospel, and preach it, in spite of all opposition.' Barnes on the witnesses' death: 'The meaning is, that there would be a period, in the progress of these events, when the cause of true religion would seem to be dead; when the witnesses for the truth would be silenced; when there would be a cessation of their testimony, as if the advocates of religion were dead and unburied.' Gill on the resurrection (v.11): 'And the breath of life from God entered into them — just as he breathed into Adam the breath of life, and he became a living soul; and the Spirit of God revived and quickened Ezekiel's valley of dry bones; so God breathed life into these witnesses; so that they revived and lived, to the great astonishment and terror of their enemies.' The two-witnesses narrative is the book's sustained account of the church's prophetic vocation: to witness even at the cost of death, and to be vindicated by resurrection — exactly as its Lord was.
1.christology: The great city of v.8, 'where their Lord was crucified,' anchors the two witnesses' story in Christ's own passion: their death in the same city (symbolically) as their Lord's links their testimony to his. The 3½ days they lie unburied (v.9) mirrors the 3 days of Christ's burial — the minimum period necessary to confirm death in ancient understanding. Their resurrection by 'a breath of life from God' (v.11) and their ascent to heaven 'in a cloud' while their enemies watch (v.12) directly pattern the disciples' experience of the resurrection and ascension (Acts 1:9). The two witnesses thus embody the Lamb's own pattern: slaughtered but standing, dead but alive, temporary defeat that becomes permanent vindication. Their ministry is explicitly theocentric in Zechariah's terms (the olive trees and lampstands standing before the Lord of the earth) but their story is christological in its shape: witness → death → resurrection → ascension → vindication. The church's prophetic witness is grounded in, patterned on, and vindicated by its Lord's own.

15.synthesis: The seventh angel sounds and loud voices in heaven cry: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever' (v.15). The twenty-four elders fall on their faces and worship: 'We give thanks to you, Lord God Almighty, who is and who was, for you have taken your great power and begun to reign. The nations raged, but your wrath came, and the time for the dead to be judged, and for rewarding your servants, the prophets and saints, and those who fear your name, both small and great, and for destroying the destroyers of the earth' (vv.17–18). Then God's temple in heaven is opened, revealing the ark of his covenant — 'and there were flashes of lightning, rumblings, peals of thunder, an earthquake, and heavy hail' (v.19).
15.historical_context: The seventh trumpet is the final trumpet and the third woe — yet its content is not a new disaster but a proclamation of accomplished victory. The announcement that 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ' (v.15) uses the aorist tense — it speaks of something accomplished, a fait accompli announced from heaven's perspective even before its full earthly manifestation. The theophanic signs that accompany the opening of the heavenly temple (v.19) are the same signs that accompanied the throne-room vision of ch.4 and will escalate through the bowl judgments. The ark of the covenant (v.19) was lost at the destruction of the first temple in 586 BCE — its heavenly appearance declares that what the earthly temple lost is preserved in heaven. The elders' thanksgiving prayer (vv.17–18) is the book's most comprehensive summary of eschatological purpose: judgment of the dead, reward of servants, and destruction of the destroyers — the complete rectification of the world's moral disorder.
15.literary_structure: The seventh trumpet (vv.15–19) functions not as a new disaster in a series but as the doxological proclamation of the entire sequence's goal. The trumpet series, which began with partial judgments affecting a third of creation, climaxes not in a greater judgment but in a heavenly declaration that the kingdom has arrived. This structural choice — ending the trumpet series with a victory hymn rather than a final plague — is theologically programmatic: the seals and trumpets are not merely disasters but purposeful disclosures of the Lamb's victory. The elders' prayer of vv.17–18 is notably past-tense ('you have taken your great power and begun to reign') — the kingdom's establishment is viewed from heaven as already accomplished at the seventh trumpet, even though the narrative will continue to describe its earthly outworking in chs.12–22. The ark's revelation (v.19) at the chapter's close points forward to the new covenant reality that will be fully disclosed in the new Jerusalem (21:22: 'I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb').
15.theological_application: Gill on 'the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord' (v.15): 'All the kingdoms of the world, which antichrist, Satan's viceroy, had usurped and got into his hands, should now return to their lawful sovereign; and he should have the dominion over them, and they should become subject to him, and be governed by his laws.' Barnes on the elders' thanksgiving: 'The idea is that the time had now come when God would "reign" in the sense of more fully manifesting his dominion, and of establishing, in a more glorious manner, a kingdom of righteousness.' The seventh trumpet's proclamation is the pastoral climax of the entire first half of Revelation: the seven churches, told to endure through tribulation, are now shown the end to which that endurance leads. The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of their Lord. The suffering of the seals, the warnings of the trumpets, the testimony of the two witnesses — all serve this end.
15.christology: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ' (v.15) — the phrase 'our Lord and his Christ' distinguishes the Father (our Lord, echoing the OT covenant formula) and Christ (the anointed one, the Messiah), yet joins them in one kingdom-claim. The reign is joint and eternal: 'he shall reign forever and ever' uses the singular ('he') for what has been announced as a shared reign, indicating the unity of Father and Son in the kingdom's governance. The elders address 'Lord God Almighty, who is and who was' (v.17) — notably omitting 'who is to come' from the threefold formula of 1:4 and 4:8, because at this point he has come: the 'coming' is no longer future but present. The ark of the covenant (v.19) is ultimately a christological symbol: the ark contained the tablets of the law, the manna, and Aaron's rod — all types of Christ (the fulfiller of the law, the bread of life, the true high priest). Its heavenly appearance at the seventh trumpet signals that all these covenant types are fulfilled in the one who reigns forever and ever.
